2606.30454.title: Collective cooperation without individual fidelity in LLM agents
2606.30454.paper_summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as agents in simulations of social systems, yet it remains unclear when their behavior can be interpreted as a faithful proxy for human decision-making. Here we test LLM agents against a direct empirical benchmark: a large-scale networked Prisoner's Dilemma experiment with human participants. Using the same interaction protocol, payoff structure, and network topologies, we compare nine open-weight LLMs with the human data. The selected model reproduces several macro-level features of cooperation dynamics, including the early decline and later stabilization of cooperation. This aggregate agreement, however, does not extend uniformly to finer levels of behavior. LLM populations underestimate individual-level heterogeneity and generate conditional cooperation patterns that differ from those observed in humans. Adding a fraction of random agents improves some aspects of micro-level agreement, but does not remove the mismatch in decision rules. These findings reveal a macro--micro dissociation in LLM-based social agents: collective outcomes can appear human-like even when the underlying behavioral distributions and mechanisms are not. They suggest that validating LLM agents as human surrogates requires comparisons across aggregate dynamics, individual heterogeneity, and context-dependent decision rules, rather than outcome-level agreement alone.
2606.30454.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）越來越多地被用作社會系統模擬中的代理，但何時可以將它們的行為解釋為人類決策的忠實代理仍不明確。<lb/>在這裡，我們將LLM代理與一個直接的實證基準進行測試：一個與人類參與者的大規模網絡囚徒困境實驗。<lb/>使用相同的互動協議、支付結構和網絡拓撲，我們將九個開放權重的LLM與人類數據進行比較。<lb/>所選模型重現了合作動態的幾個宏觀特徵，包括合作的早期下降和後期穩定。<lb/>然而，這種整體一致性並未均勻延伸到更細微的行為層面。<lb/>LLM群體低估了個體層面的異質性，並產生了與人類觀察到的不同的條件合作模式。<lb/>增加一部分隨機代理改善了一些微觀層面的一致性，但並未消除決策規則上的不匹配。<lb/>這些發現揭示了基於LLM的社會代理中的宏觀-微觀分離：即使在基礎行為分佈和機制不相同的情況下，集體結果也可能看起來類似人類。<lb/>它們表明，驗證LLM代理作為人類替代品需要在整體動態、個體異質性和上下文依賴的決策規則之間進行比較，而不僅僅是結果層面的協議。
2606.30454.author: Henrique Ferraz de Arruda et.al.
2606.30454.authors: Henrique Ferraz de Arruda, Carlos Gracia Lázaro, Alberto Aleta, Yamir Moreno
